topic: FFI - CStr and CString: Null-Terminated String Safety

turns.content: Hey, I'm trying to call a legacy C function from our Rust wrapper but got this error: NulError at offset 5. My code looks like it's just passing a string directly, so why is the null terminator thing breaking me?

That NulError happens because C APIs expect null-terminated strings, and your Rust string isn't automatically null-terminated by default. When you create a CString with something like CString::new("hello") it allocates memory and stores the null byte at the end, but if you try to convert that raw pointer or slice into a CStr without ensuring the zero-byte exists at the boundary, the C function reads past your actual data. The key is understanding that CStr requires a visible nul byte before you can safely hand off ownership of those bytes to unsafe C code.
So I shouldn't be using as_ptr on just any string slice? What's the right way to bridge this gap when I need zero-copy access without allocating?

Exactly, you want zero-copy from static data or known buffers. If your source is a compile-time constant like b"text\0", use CStr::from_bytes_with_nul(b"text") which creates an immutable CStr directly without touching the heap. For dynamic strings that already have a nul terminator in memory, CString::new("text") will allocate and store that nul byte internally so you can safely call to_str() later. But remember that as_ptr only gives you a raw pointer; if you hand that pointer into unsafe C code, the lifetime of your Rust string must outlive that entire C call or you'll get undefined behavior.
